# From First Block Caching to Speculative Caching in Diffusion Models

## Introduction

In this article, we explore how to achieve significant inference speedups in Flow Matching models, with a focus on FLUX. Rather than diving deep into the mathematical foundations of diffusion or flow matching, I'll concentrate on reducing computation and accelerating inference while maintaining generation quality.

## High-level Explanation of Diffusion Model

**![](data:image/png;base64,)**
credit: https://medium.com/@steinsfu/diffusion-model-clearly-explained-cd331bd41166

At a high level, a diffusion model works by progressively adding noise to an image until it becomes pure noise, then learning to reverse that process, recovering a coherent image by iteratively denoising the latent representation. During this process, the model learns how to generate data from noise to actual distribution.

For this post, we focus specifically on the inference side of diffusion models and more precisely, flow matching inference.

## Diffusion Inference vs LLM Inference

_(Note: "LLM inference" here excludes Text Diffusion / Diffusion LLMs, which blend language modeling with diffusion-style generation.)_

Diffusion models require dozens to hundreds of sequential denoising steps at generation time, each demanding a full forward pass through the model (two-level sequential). This makes diffusion inference fundamentally compute-bound, in contrast to LLM inference, which tends to be memory-bound due to the cost of loading KV caches and weights.

Thus memory bandwidth is less of a bottleneck for diffusion than for LLMs. To meaningfully accelerate diffusion inference, we need to reduce the raw amount of computation, which is often more limited than memory-bound problems.

## Two Approaches in Accelerating Diffusion Inference

Currently there are two most widely used paths in order to accelerate Diffusion inference which is model compression or cache reuse.

![[Pasted image 20260519165449.png]]

**Model Compression method** refers to making the neural network smaller which can be distilling the parent model, token reduction, quantization, and pruning. However it is very hard to preserve the same quality of the original model compared to the compressed model, especially if it's aggressively compressed (if we could preserve 100%, why would we use the original model anyway). 

![[Pasted image 20260519165837.png]]
Figure: diminishing quality (credit: https://blog.squeezebits.com/77516)
As we reduce the model size, however, the cost of expressive power and accuracy declines. It also cannot handle negative prompt Also we need to retrain the original model in order to use this method. A case study at SqueezeBits team(https://blog.squeezebits.com/77516) shows this very well.

**Feature Caching methods** (notably FBCache, TeaCache, and TaylorSeer etc.) are adopted to Diffusion libraries such as HuggingFace Diffusers or Omni inference engines such as Max. These methods use features of the previous timestep (e.g., attention, MLP representation, etc.) and try to reuse or forecast it if possible. We do not need to retrain a separate model. However this method as well cannot avoid the tradeoff between computational efficiency vs image quality.

We will look at two examples of these caching methods. First, there is a caching method called FBCache. 

### FBCache

FBCache(First Block Cache)(https://github.com/chengzeyi/ParaAttention/blob/7a266123671b55e7e5a2fe9af3121f07a36afc78/README.md#first-block-cache-our-dynamic-caching) is an idea to _detect_ when the change is likely to be minor and skip the later blocks of the denoiser.

![[Pasted image 20260509102721.png]]
Figure: from [Semih Berkay Öztürk's blogpost](https://semihb.com/)

In order to do this, we run the first block of the step. Usually each step is consisted of multiple blocks (either U-net or Transformer). This can be represented as:
$$f_{\theta}(x_t) = f_{\mathrm{rest}} \left(f_{\mathrm{first}}(x_t)\right)$$

If the difference between $h_t = f_{\mathrm{first}}(x_t)$ and the input $x_t$ is smaller than threshold($\left\| h_t - h_{t+1} \right\| < \text{threshold}$), we consider the next timestep will be similar to the current time step thus skip the $f_\text{rest}$ computation and reuse the previously cached final output.

$$f_{\mathrm{rest}}(h_t) \approx f_{\mathrm{rest}}(h_{t+1})$$

This intuitively makes sense because not every time step in a diffusion model's denoising process requires the same amount of computation. Only some steps produce large changes, while others make only minor adjustments(https://semihb.com/essays/first-block-caching/) to the latent.

Using the first Transformer block's output however is just a heuristic instead of a mathematical guarantee, so the quality of the image may vary based on how high the threshold is. As noted in the SqueezeBits Blog post, while FBCache does not harm output, it has speedup limits on up to 2~3x. Beyond that will lead to lower image quality. For example, the background gets blurry.

### TaylorSeer

Another example is TaylorSeer, which uses Taylor series-based approximation to predict multi-step evolution of intermediate features. Unlike previous caching methods such as FBCache, which simply reuse a previously computed feature as an approximation for the current step, TaylorSeer explicitly forecasts how the feature will change over future timesteps.

This relies on the observation that features in diffusion models often change slowly and continuously across adjacent timesteps.

![[Pasted image 20260515080523.png]]

As shown in the figure above, both the features and their derivatives follow a relatively smooth trajectory, without significantly random changes. TaylorSeer assumes that feature representations evolve smoothly over time, making it possible to extrapolate nearby future features from previous feature states.

### Taylor Series

**Taylor Series** is a calculus idea for approximating an unknown function $\mathcal{F}$ near a known point $x=a$. Instead of figuring out the entire function exactly, we use the function value and its derivative(s) at $a$ to build an $m$-th order polynomial approximation. Intuitively, if we know the current value, its change rate, and higher-order change patterns, we can estimate how the function will behave slightly before or after the current point $a$.

>[!Note]
>From here, we will use $\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{pred}}$ as the n-dimension polynomial used for approximating the actual function $\mathcal{F}$.

Intuitively, the more information we have about how the feature changes, the better we can approximate its future value.

For example, a second-order approximation that uses both the slope and curvature can capture the trajectory more accurately than a first-order approximation that only uses the slope.

Also, prediction becomes more reliable when the target timestep is closer, because the feature trajectory changes less over a short interval than over a long interval.

I built a [TaylorSeer playground](http://junupark.xyz/taylorseer-playground/) where you can adjust the two knobs:
- $N$, which controls how aggressively caching is applied (target timestep is further) and
- $m$, which is used to tune the order of the Taylor approximation.

By changing these values, you can directly see how accuracy and compute cost changes.

### Directly Caching ($m=0$)

Let's start with naive feature reuse. This can be interpreted as a $0$-th order Taylor approximation, where we assume the feature does not change over the cached interval: 

$$\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{pred}}(a + N) =\mathcal{F}(a) = k$$

In a 2D graph, this simply means extending the current feature value as a flat horizontal line.

However the actual feature trajectory continues to evolve across diffusion timesteps, so directly reusing the previous feature can introduce approximation error.

When the cache interval is small, such as $N=3$, the gap between the reused feature and the actual feature may remain small. But when caching is applied more aggressively, such as $N=7$, this gap can grow significantly and may degrade image quality.

### Linear Prediction Method ($m=1$)

The simplest forecasting method is a first-order, or linear prediction. Instead of directly reusing the cached feature, we estimate how the feature is moving and extrapolate it $k$ steps ahead. The authors refer to this as _short-term forecasting_.

$$\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{pred}}\left(x_{t-k}^{l}\right)
=
\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
+
\frac{
\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
-
\mathcal{F}\left(x_{t+N}^{l}\right)
}{N}
\, k$$
where $x_t^{l}$ is the current timestep and $k$ is the step we are trying to forecast. Since $\frac{\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)-\mathcal{F}\left(x_{t+N}^{l}\right)}{N}\,$ is the slope, we multiply it by $k$ to predict the time after $k$ steps.

Unlike cache reuse, where we only needed to cache the latent feature, we need to additionally save the derivative of latent feature based on the prediction function.

$$

C\left(x_t^{l}\right)
:=
\left\{
\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right),
\Delta \mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
\right\}$$
where
$$\Delta \mathcal{F}(x_t^l) = \mathcal{F}(x_t^l) - \mathcal{F}(x_{t+N}^l) \approx N \times \mathcal{F}'(x^l_t)$$
In TaylorSeer, **we do not calculate the derivative directly**($\mathcal{F}', \mathcal{F}''$) because it's expensive and we would need to do some sort of back propagation on the latent features, which increases amount to compute. Instead, we use the full calculated features before to approximate (간이) derivative. As the Taylor order $m$ increases, we need more ($m$) previous features.

### Higher-Order Prediction ($m \geq 2$) via Taylor Expansion

Using Taylor expansion, we can scale the prediction function to 2, 3, 4 dimension and beyond. 

The $i$-th order forward finite difference can be defined as
$$\Delta^{i}\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
=
\Delta\left(
\Delta^{i-1}\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
\right)
=
\Delta^{i-1}\mathcal{F}\left(x_{t+N}^{l}\right)
-
\Delta^{i-1}\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)$$


This will require us a more complex prediction function resulting in more compute, but will reduce the error rate as well.

$$\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{pred},m}\left(x_{t-k}^{l}\right)
=
\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
+
\sum_{i=1}^{m}
\frac{
\Delta^{i}\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
}{
i! \, N^{i}
}
(-k)^i$$
where $N$ is the gap between every full forward.


As an intuitive example, if we want to get 3rd taylor order, we need four values: F0, F1, F2, F3.
first order finite difference: D0 = F1-F0, D1 = F2-F1, D2 = F3-F2
second order finite difference: E0 = D1-D0, E2 = D2-D1
third order finite difference: G0 = E1-E0

If we generalize this, we need $i+1$ values to calculate $i$-order finite difference. Thus we additionally save the $m(m \geq 2)$ derivatives needed depending on the taylor order we chose.

$$C\left(x_t^{l}\right)
:=
\left\{
\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right),
\Delta \mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right),
\ldots,
\Delta^{m}\mathcal{F}\left(x_t^{l}\right)
\right\}$$


You can check how the quality, speedup, amount of compute changes by checking out the playground. (Playground is based on the ablation study and experiment presented in the paper).

One caveat of this method is that it lacks of validation mechanism. So while it is known to accelerate the inference, there is no guarantee in prediction accuracy. Especially as the timestep between full forward pass increases and more cache forecast used between, the error is likely to increase and this can end up degrading the quality of image.

While it is experimented that using caching method lightly can bring up 2x speedup, if we try to reduce compute too aggressively, it can also degrade performance, which is also something user wants to avoid. There is a tradeoff/tension between reducing the amount of compute and latency vs preserving the quality of the image.

Another problem in existing approaches is that they apply the same acceleration ratio to all samples, maintaining the same sampling intervals statically. (TODO: add example)

To summarize what we went so far,
- diffusion models require high sequential, computational costs.
- So we want to reduce the burden while preserving the quality
- However existing methods (including TaylorSeer) have limitations in preserving the quality of image, if it is too aggressively tuned.

## SpeCa: Forecast with Verification for Correctness

The previous caching methods can be summarized as _"cache-then-verify"_ paradigm while TaylorSeer introduces _"cache-then-forecast"_.

SpeCa is a method can be called a _"forecast-then-verify"_ paradigm where we use TaylorSeer but try to reduce the error rate significantly by verifying.

(1) Execute a complete forward computation at key time step $t$ (I will explain what _key_ means later) to obtain accurate feature representation ${f}(x^l_t)$
(2) predict the features for subsequent $k$ times steps $\{t-1, t-2, ... , t-k\}$ using TaylorSeer, where $k$ is the parameter of speculation step.
(3) Evaluate the reliability of the predicted features through validation method and decide whether to accept or reject each prediction step.
